Name: jacobian_layer_skipping
Title: Jacobian-Based Layer Skipping: Training-Free Latency-Aware Inference via Spectral Stability Bounds
Short Hypothesis: Jacobian-based spectral stability metrics can be used to dynamically skip layers during inference without significant accuracy loss, enabling strict latency-aware execution under bounded computational budgets.
Related Work: Existing layer-skipping methods often rely on heuristic thresholds or require expensive training for skip policies. Spectral Latent Steering (SLS) and Spectral Quantization Routing (SQR) have explored spectral properties for test-time optimization and precision allocation, but neither addresses layer skipping directly. Our proposal uniquely combines Jacobian-based spectral stability analysis with PAC-Bayes bounds to derive a training-free, latency-aware layer-skipping framework.
Abstract: Layer skipping has emerged as a promising technique for reducing inference latency in deep neural networks. However, existing methods often rely on heuristic thresholds or require costly training for skip policies, limiting their practicality and scalability. We propose Jacobian-Based Layer Skipping (JBLS), a training-free framework that dynamically skips layers based on Jacobian spectral stability metrics. We hypothesize that layers with well-conditioned Jacobians and rapidly decaying spectral norms are less critical for inference and can be safely skipped without significant accuracy degradation. JBLS computes these metrics on-the-fly using randomized SVD, mapping them to a skip policy that enforces strict computational budgets. We theoretically ground JBLS with PAC-Bayes bounds that link Jacobian spectral stability to generalization stability under layer skipping. Evaluated on single-GPU setups across WILDS benchmarks, JBLS achieves a superior throughput-accuracy Pareto frontier compared to static and heuristic layer-skipping baselines. By replacing qualitative fragility claims with formalized risk bounds, JBLS provides a transparent, reproducible pathway for resource-efficient inference under natural distribution shifts.
Experiments: Benchmark JBLS against static and heuristic layer-skipping baselines on WILDS datasets using throughput-accuracy Pareto frontiers.
Evaluate JBLS on single-GPU setups to validate its practicality and scalability.
Measure the impact of Jacobian spectral stability metrics on inference accuracy and latency.
Validate PAC-Bayes bounds by comparing theoretical risk bounds with empirical generalization stability under layer skipping.
Risk Factors and Limitations: Potential for increased computational overhead due to on-the-fly Jacobian spectral stability computations.
Risk of significant accuracy degradation if Jacobian spectral stability metrics are not well-calibrated.
Limitations in scalability to larger models or more complex tasks due to computational constraints.
